title: Who’s right about regime change at the Fed: K. Warsh for pursuing it or Jay Powell for resisting it?

Bullard. Uh, so what what I'm showing
here in the blue line is the OIS derived
neutral rate. Uh, so we take the in a
cutting cycle, we take the floor of Fed
funds rate as the proxy for the neutral
the market's expectation of neutral. In
a hiking cycle, we'll take the terminal
Fed funds rate five years out as a proxy
for the neutral rate. And so right now,
we're pretty much close. The effective
funds rate is almost, you know, eight
basis points away from the
OIS derived neutral rate. So, in our
opinion, we think the Fed would be wise
to sit on its hands here and not to give
us a repeat of 2020 and 2021, which led
to the nationwide affordability crisis
that Chair Powell, as wonderful as a man
as he is,
I was really disappointed with him
yesterday but not for the reason
everybody else is disappointed with him.
I think he's actually making a good
decision on that.
Very disappointed with him for still
still five years later still refusing to
take accountability for their outsized
contributions to our ongoing nationwide
affordability crisis, which has
destroyed the lives of millions and many
millions of families across this
country.
The government the federal government
accumulated $6 trillion in deficits in
2020 and 2021. Big problem for

what's a bigger problem. The Fed
monetized almost 60% of that and kept
the policy rate 1,000 basis points below
what the Taylor rule said it should have
been in 2020 and 2021.
I didn't hear him say anything about
that for the past five years. Just still
blaming pandemic supply chains, Russia's
invasion of Ukraine. Darius has got a
purple tie on, so that's 10 basis points
on inflation. Like, come on, man. Geez
Louise. The country deserves better.
Divergent signals from labor supply and
labor demand make it difficult to assess
whether hawks or doves currently have a
better read on the labor market. So
right now,
if you look at labor supply, it's
collapsing at minus 3.3%, so that's
hawkish. But if you look at labor
demand, it's also collapsing at minus 3%
year-over-year in terms of private
sector job openings. So, what's right?
I don't
this this is a very open debate and and
open debate August 4th, no change in
policy.
Divergent signals from the U3 headline
unemployment rate and the long-term
unemployment rate make it difficult to
assess whether hawks or doves currently
have a better read on the labor market.
So, the U3 unemployment rate the
headline unemployment rate is well below
its long-run mean of 5.7% at 4.3%. And
then the long-term unemployment rate as
a percent of total is well above its
long-run mean of 16.5% with the latest
value of 25.4%. So, who's right? Uh,
it's hard to know right now. But so that
ultimately August 4th, no change in
policy.
Divergent signals from low productivity
labor demand and high productivity labor
demand make it difficult to assess
whether hawks or doves currently have a
better read on the labor market. So, we
can see that there's a tremendous amount
of low productivity labor demand in the
US economy when you look at the percent
of cumulative growth of non-farm
payrolls coming from government or
government adjacent sectors, which we
said earlier include federal, state,
local governments, health care,
education, and social services.
149% of all the job growth that has come
in the Trump 2.0 economy has been from
the government or government adjacent
sector, which obviously means the high
productivity labor demand from the true
private economy is collapsing. It's
going down.
And so that's a big problem. And so that
it makes it difficult for the Fed to
assess, okay, if you have a lot of low
productivity labor demand, that's
ultimately very that compresses the
nationwide margin, which ultimately
should going back to that chart at the
end of the inflation cycle, should take
the green line up in a way that supports
inflation. But if you have a
>> [clears throat]
>> collapse in high productivity labor
demand, then you're suggesting that the
Fed needs to get out there and do
something about it. So, the doves should
win that debate. It's a hard debate to
win. I don't know who's going to win
right now. But I think over the next one
to two years, it'll become very obvious
to the policy makers that we're in a
jobless recovery and a productivity boom
that gives them scope to ease policy
further. But right now,
in our opinion, I think doing nothing is
probably the best idea. The best because
they don't really need to, right? We're
talking about economy that's going to
grow well above trend this year for
right on our growth thesis. So,
divergent signals from high income real
wage growth and low income real wage
growth make it difficult to assess
whether hawks or doves currently have a
better read on the labor market. So, we
had
basically low income real wage growth
has gotten more than cut in half in the
Trump 2.0 economy relative to what it
was in the last two years of the Biden
administration. But high income real
wage growth has accelerated modestly to
1.8%. So, again,
if you're hawkish, you're focusing on
the acceleration in high income wage
growth and the the purchasing power that
provides. If you're dovish, you're
focusing on the utter collapse in real
wage growth for workers in the first
quartile in this in the Trump 2.0
economy and suggesting that, "Hey, you
need to support those on the low
income." So again, very difficult
debate. Kevin Warsh will be having this
debate with his colleagues on the FOMC.
And I think they're going to do a lot of
debating and a whole lot of nothing in
the in the coming months.
We agree with the incoming Fed chair,
Kevin Warsh, that a productivity boom
creates scope to lower the policy rate
further.
His colleagues on the FOMC generally do
not. When you look at the most recent
dot plot from March 18th, the Fed is
essentially starting to signal and
suggest that that they agree with our
call that we're going to we're heading
into a productivity boom, not in
absolute terms, but in delta and rate of
change terms. And then they they took up
their estimate of neutral alongside
that. So, they're essentially saying
productivity boom equals higher neutral,
higher R-star. Kevin Warsh is saying it
equals lower neutral, lower R-star, as
is Stephen Roach,
as is Rick Reader. I'm in the
Roach-Reader-Warsh camp. We are here at
42 Macro.
You know, if you're going to be in a
camp, I would much rather be in their
camp than the camp of the folks who let
the inflation genie out of the bottle,
who were too late in 2018 or too late in
2020 or too late in 2023 and were too
late in 2025.
History indicates that a productivity
boom would create scope for additional
rate cuts that would likely feature
limited pass-through to the bond market.
So, if you look at the median value of
the year-over-year rate of change of the
two-year nominal Treasury yield and the
10-year nominal Treasury yield with data
going back to '77 and '63, respectively,
when you're going from a 1 to 2% trend
productivity economy, you typically have
a Treasury yield that's rising on a
median basis about 10%. And then when
you go to a 3 to 4% trend productivity
economy, again, we're calling for at
least a 150 basis point trend
acceleration in productivity growth.
We've already seen that happen in the
'90s and 2000s with a much less
productivity-enhancing technology than
AI. So, we think that's in the bag. It
might be 200 basis point trend
acceleration in productivity growth,
especially if you have the jobs
declining
as they have as they are with true
private services currently. So, we're
transitioning from a condition where the
two-year nominal Treasury yield is a
proxy for short rates
tends to rise to a condition where the
two-year nominal Treasury yield tends to
fall. So, you basically once you get out
of the one to two anything below 2%
trend productivity with data going back
to 1977, it's rates the short rates tend
to fall. The market likes productivity
growth and essentially says it gives the
Fed scope to to to ease policy. This is
what Greenspan figured out well before
everyone else. And so essentially Kevin
Warsh is going to copy and paste what he
learned from Alan Greenspan and what he
learned from Kevin Hassett and what he
learned from the supply-side economist,
our friend John Taylor over at the
Hoover Institution, etc. Obviously, Stan
Druckenmiller is someone who I I had a
tremendous amount of respect for. I
think he's probably the best fundamental
investor in in world history.
You know, he's he's going to take some
some really important insights that this
chart sort of really embellishes to the
Federal Reserve and hopefully changes
the debate.
Cuz I tell you right now, the people
gasping for economic oxygen with
essentially an all-time high auto loan
delinquency rate, credit card
delinquency rate, student loan
delinquency rate right now.

financial crisis.

support, especially in the context of

going up a whole dollar in the last six
